desc: The most abundant bumble bee in western North America. Distinctive yellow face and anterior thoracic band on otherwise black body. Primary buzz pollinator of tomatoes (vibrates flowers to release pollen) and critical native wildflower pollinator.

fm.Thorax: Black with bright yellow anterior band
fm.Abdomen: T1 yellow; T2–T3 black; T4 bright yellow; T5–T6 black
fm.Hair: Bright lemon-yellow on black; clean contrast
fm.Face: Yellow; short rounded; short tongue (¼ in–⅓ in)
fm.Size: Queens ⅔ in–¾ in; workers ⅓ in–⅔ in

hp: Buzz pollinator of native Penstemon, Salvia, Phacelia, Clarkia. Generalist on many flowers. Largest SoCal bumblebee. Stable population.

desc: Large, long-tongued species in substantial decline since the 1990s. Critical pollinator for deep-corolla wildflowers that shorter-tongued bees cannot service. Queens among the largest bumble bees. Black with variable yellow thoracic bands.

fm.Thorax: Yellow anterior; broad black between wings
fm.Abdomen: T1 yellow; T2–T3 yellow/black; T4 tawny
fm.Hair: Rich golden; some populations tawny T3–T4
fm.Face: Black facial hair; long tongue (⅓ in–½ in)
fm.Size: Queens ¾ in–1 in (among largest CA Bombus)

hp: Long-tongued buzz pollinator of deep-flowered natives — Penstemon, Salvia, Clarkia, Delphinium. In severe decline since 1990s (Hatfield et al. 2015).

desc: CESA candidate endangered (2019 petition). Dramatic collapse — pesticides, habitat loss, pathogen spillover. Every iNat obscritical.

fm.Thorax: Yellow anterior; extensive black; thin yellow posterior
fm.Abdomen: T1 yellow; T2–T3 tawny-orange (diagnostic); T4 orange-brown
fm.Hair: Warm orange-brown unique in LA County Bombus
fm.Size: Queens ⅔ in–1 in; workers ½ in–⅔ in

hp: CESA candidate endangered. Pollinator of Penstemon, Clarkia, Phacelia. 98% population decline (Hatfield et al. 2015, IUCN). Pesticides and pathogens.

desc: Robust desert-adapted species tolerant of extreme heat. Active in late afternoon when other bees have retreated. Important pollinator of desert wildflowers. Yellow-and-black pattern.

fm.Thorax: Extensively yellow; narrow black between wings
fm.Abdomen: T1–T4 predominantly yellow; T5 mixed; T6 black
fm.Face: Yellow; broad rounded; medium tongue (⅓ in)
fm.Size: Queens ⅔ in–¾ in; workers ½ in–⅔ in

hp: Pollinator of native wildflowers. Desert habitat.

desc: Polymorphic species with both orange-banded and yellow-banded morphs occurring in LA County. Higher elevations in San Gabriel and Santa Monica Mountains. Important early-season pollinator emerging before most other Bombus species.

fm.Thorax: Yellow or orange anterior band; black centrally
fm.Abdomen: T1–T2 orange or yellow; T3–T6 black (diagnostic "black tail")
fm.Hair: Orange morph: vivid T1–T2. Yellow morph: lemon bands.
fm.Size: Queens ⅔ in; workers ⅓ in–½ in

hp: Early-season montane buzz pollinator — among first bees flying each spring. Polymorphic (orange and yellow morphs). San Gabriel and Santa Monica Mountains. On Ribes, Ceanothus, Arctostaphylos.

desc: At extreme southern range edge in LA County, restricted to San Gabriel Mountain meadows above 4,000 ft. Yellow-haired with single dark abdominal band. Declining range-wide. Once transcontinental, now increasingly fragmented. IUCN Vulnerable.

fm.Thorax: Entirely bright yellow with minimal black
fm.Hair: Richest golden-yellow of any LA Bombus
fm.Face: Yellow; elongated; long tongue (⅓ in–½ in)
fm.Size: Queens ⅔ in–¾ in; workers ½ in–⅔ in

hp: Pollinator of native wildflowers and crops. At extreme southern range edge — San Gabriel Mountains only.

desc: Once common across North America, now in severe continent-wide decline. LA County records increasingly sparse. Yellow thorax with single dark abdominal band. Conservation concern.

fm.Thorax: Yellow anterior; broad black behind wings
fm.Abdomen: T1 yellow; T2 mixed; T3–T6 black
fm.Hair: Pale yellow with extensive black; muted
fm.Size: Queens ⅔ in–1 in; workers ½ in–⅔ in

hp: American bumblebee — continent-wide decline exceeding 90% since 2000 (Cameron et al. 2011, PNAS). LA records now exceedingly rare.

desc: Distinctively pale bumble bee of arid western habitats. Paler than any other LA County Bombus. On flowers in desert scrub and chaparral margins.

fm.Abdomen: T1 pale; T2–T3 black; T4–T5 white/cream (pale tail)
fm.Size: Queens ⅔ in–¾ in; workers ½ in

hp: Arid-adapted bumblebee of desert scrub and chaparral margins. Distinctively pale — whitish/cream banding. Buzz pollinator of desert wildflowers including Larrea and Sphaeralcea.

desc: Cryptic species easily confused with B. vosnesenskii. Distinguished by darker, duller yellow, dark facial hair, and rounded malar space. IUCN Vulnerable. Confirmed sightings are valuable conservation data — photograph and report.

fm.Abdomen: T1 narrower yellow; T2–T3 black; T4 reduced; T5–T6 black
fm.Size: Queens ⅔ in; workers ⅓ in–½ in

hp: Montane meadows. IUCN Vulnerable — cryptic species confused with B.
